---
title: "ANOVA"
---

<span class="section-badge">Basics</span>

Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) extends the logic of the t-test to situations with three or more groups, multiple factors, covariates, or repeated measurements. It answers the question: are there significant differences in means across conditions?

This page covers four ANOVA designs. Jump to the relevant section:

- [One-way ANOVA](#one-way-anova) — one factor, three or more independent groups
- [ANCOVA](#ancova-analysis-of-covariance) — one factor with one or more continuous covariates
- [Factorial ANOVA](#factorial-anova) — two or more factors (between-subjects)
- [Repeated-measures ANOVA](#repeated-measures-anova) — within-subjects or mixed designs

---

## One-way ANOVA

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## Research vignette — do the four Hogwarts houses differ in Machiavellianism?
Jakob et al. (2019) measured Machiavellianism in students who identified as Gryffindor, Hufflepuff, Ravenclaw, or Slytherin — four independent groups on one continuous personality scale. A one-way ANOVA tests whether *any* house differs from the others; a planned contrast (Slytherin vs the rest) tests the obvious sequel. Source: Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 11).

→ Worked through in [Exercise 1 below](#exercise-1-one-way-anova-do-the-four-hogwarts-houses-differ-in-machiavellianism).
:::

### What is it?

One-way ANOVA tests whether the means of three or more independent groups differ on a continuous dependent variable. The "one-way" refers to a single grouping factor (independent variable).

The F-statistic is the ratio of *between-groups variance* to *within-groups variance*. A large F indicates that the group means are more spread out than expected by chance.

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Where the F-statistic comes from
F is the ratio of the mean squares of the *model* (how much variance the group means capture) to the mean squares of the *error* (leftover within-group variance). Like a *t*-statistic, F is a standardised test statistic with a known sampling distribution — that's how a *p*-value gets attached to it.
:::

### Assumptions

::: {.assumption-list}
- **Independence**: each participant contributes data to only one group
- **Normality**: the DV should be approximately normally distributed *within each group* (check with Q-Q plot of residuals)
- **Homogeneity of variance (homoscedasticity)**: the variance should be similar across groups — check with Levene's test or observed group variances. If violated, use Welch's ANOVA (available in JASP)
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Why assumptions actually matter (small demonstration)
When the DV is heavy-tailed (e.g. reaction times with extreme values), the empirical sampling distribution of *F* under H₀ no longer matches the theoretical *F*-distribution. The result: your α and power aren't what you think they are. ANOVA is relatively robust to mild non-normality, but vulnerable to extreme observations that inflate within-group variance.

→ [Simulation: what 100,000 ANOVAs under H₀ actually look like](https://johnnydoorn.github.io/teaching-statistics/extra-texts/ViolatingAssumptionsANOVA.html)
:::

### Effect size

Report **η² (eta squared)** or the less biased **ω² (omega squared)**. For factorial designs, make sure to use the partial versions of these effect sizes (partial η² & partial ω²).

| η² | Conventional label |
|---|---|
| .01 | Small |
| .06 | Medium |
| .14 | Large |

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Effect size vs. test statistic — what's the difference?
A *t* (or *F*) statistic grows with both the magnitude of the effect *and* the sample size — increase *n* with a fixed mean difference and *t* climbs accordingly. Effect sizes like Cohen's *d*, *r*, or η² are standardised and largely insulated from sample size. That's why a significant result with a tiny effect size can still be practically meaningless, and why both belong in your report.

→ [Worked example with numbers](https://johnnydoorn.github.io/teaching-statistics/extra-texts/EffSizeVsTestStat.html)
:::

### Post-hoc tests

A significant F-test only tells you that *at least one pair of groups differs* — not which pairs. Request post-hoc tests (e.g., Tukey HSD) or planned contrasts to identify specific differences. JASP provides both.

### How to do it in JASP

Go to **ANOVA → ANOVA** in the analysis menu.

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## JASP Video Library — One-Way ANOVA
Recordings at [jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#anova-oneway](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#anova-oneway):

- [▶ Main Analysis](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#main-analysis) — setting up one-way ANOVA, post-hoc tests
- [▶ Normality and Homogeneity Check](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#normality-and-homogeneity-check) — assumption checks
- [▶ Effect Size](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#effect-size) — requesting η² and ω²
- [▶ Descriptive Plots](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#descriptive-plots) — visualising group means
:::

---

## ANCOVA (Analysis of Covariance)

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## Research vignette — does interpretation training induce a lasting negative bias in children?
Muris et al. (2008) randomly assigned children to one of three training conditions (negative / positive / no information about an ambiguous scenario) and later measured an interpretation-bias score. Age, gender, and natural anxiety differ between children and would muddy a plain one-way ANOVA; an ANCOVA adjusts for those covariates so the comparison between training conditions sits on equal footing. Source: Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 12).

→ Worked through in [Exercise 2 below](#exercise-2-ancova-does-interpretation-training-induce-a-persistent-negative-bias-in-children).
:::

### What is it?

ANCOVA extends ANOVA by adding one or more **continuous covariates** — variables you want to statistically control for. By removing the variance explained by the covariate, ANCOVA gives a more sensitive test of the group effect.

**Example**: comparing therapy outcomes across three groups while accounting for pre-therapy symptom severity.

### Key additional assumption

- **Homogeneity of regression slopes**: the relationship between the covariate and the DV should be the same in all groups. JASP can test this by adding a Group × Covariate [interaction](#what-interaction-really-means) — it should not be significant. If the interaction effect is significant, you need to take this into account when interpreting the main effect of group. For instance, this therapy is effective for people in group A, *if they have low pre-therapy symptom severity*. *(Unfamiliar with what an "interaction" is? See the callout in the Factorial ANOVA section below.)*

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## How ANCOVA partitions variance (with worked example)
Once you add a covariate, the regression coefficients for the group dummies no longer recover the raw group means — they recover means *adjusted for* the covariate. The F-tests for group and covariate then come from comparing the full model against reduced models that drop one predictor at a time.

→ [Worked example: extraversion ~ nationality + openness](https://johnnydoorn.github.io/teaching-statistics/extra-texts/PredictingWithDummies_1Variable.html)
:::

### How to do it in JASP

Add covariates in the **Covariates** box when setting up an ANOVA.

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## JASP Video Library — ANCOVA
Recordings at [jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#ancova](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#ancova):

- [▶ Main Analysis](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#main-analysis-2) — setting up ANCOVA, adding the covariate
- [▶ Add Interaction](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#add-interaction) — testing homogeneity of regression slopes
- [▶ Descriptive Plots](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#descriptives-plots) — marginal means after controlling for the covariate
:::

---

## Factorial ANOVA

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## Research vignette — does mood × "stop rule" affect compulsive checking?
Davey et al. (2003) crossed two factors: induced mood (negative vs positive) × stop-rule for an ambiguous task ("as many as can" / "feel like continuing" / no instruction), then measured how much each participant checked. The interaction — does the effect of mood depend on which stop-rule you've been given? — is the substantive question; the two main effects on their own can be misleading when the interaction is the real story. Source: Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 13).

→ Worked through in [Exercise 3 below](#exercise-3-factorial-anova-does-mood-stop-rule-affect-checking-behaviour).
:::

### What is it?

Factorial ANOVA includes **two or more between-subjects factors**. In addition to the main effect of each factor, it tests the **interaction** — whether the effect of one factor depends on the level of another.

**Example**: testing whether a new intervention is effective (factor 1: intervention vs. control) and whether this depends on participant gender (factor 2: male/female/other).

### Interpreting interactions

An interaction means the effect of factor A is different at different levels of factor B. Always examine interaction plots — JASP provides these under *Descriptive plots*. A significant interaction means you should interpret simple main effects rather than main effects alone.

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true #what-interaction-really-means}
## What "interaction" really means
A concrete example: *does the effect of alcohol on driving ability depend on the dose of speed (methamphetamine) the driver has also taken?* Alcohol on its own impairs driving, and so does speed on its own — but the question of whether their *combined* effect is more (or less) than the sum of their separate effects is what the interaction term tests. If somebody who's taken a high dose of speed shows a *bigger* alcohol-related impairment than someone who hasn't, that's the interaction: "the effect of one variable depends on the level of another".
:::

### How to do it in JASP

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## JASP Video Library — Factorial ANOVA
Recordings at [jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#anova-factorial](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#anova-factorial):

- [▶ Main Analysis](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#main-analysis-1) — setting up a two-way ANOVA
- [▶ Plots](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#plots) — interaction plots
- [▶ Simple Main Effects](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#simple-main-effects) — follow-up after a significant interaction
- [▶ Post-Hoc Tests](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#post-hoc-tests) — pairwise comparisons
:::

---

## Repeated-measures ANOVA

::: {.callout-tip icon=false}
## Research vignette — are splattered cadavers distracting during serial recall?
Each participant attempted serial recall under three different music conditions (no music / classical / death metal with explicit lyrics) — three measurements per person, so the music factor is *within-subjects*. A repeated-measures ANOVA exploits the within-person correlation to ask whether the genre actually shifts memory performance, and Mauchly's test / Greenhouse-Geisser correction handle the sphericity assumption that comes with three or more levels. Source: Labcoat Leni (DSUJ ch. 14).

→ Worked through in [Exercise 4 below](#exercise-4-repeated-measures-anova-are-splattered-cadavers-distracting-during-serial-recall).
:::

### What is it?

Repeated-measures ANOVA is used when the **same participants are measured across multiple conditions or time points** (within-subjects design). Because measurements within a person are correlated, this design is more powerful than a between-subjects design of the same size.

**Mixed designs** contain both a within-subjects factor (e.g., time: pre, post, follow-up) and a between-subjects factor (e.g., treatment group).

### Key additional assumption: sphericity

For within-subjects factors with **three or more levels**, ANOVA assumes **sphericity** — that the variances of the differences between all pairs of levels are equal. JASP performs **Mauchly's test** automatically:

- If sphericity is violated (*p* < .05): use the **Greenhouse-Geisser** or **Huynh-Feldt** correction (JASP offers both)
- If there are only two within-subjects levels, sphericity cannot be violated (it is automatic)

::: {.callout-tip collapse=true}
## Sphericity, Mauchly, and "just always apply the correction"
Sphericity = the variances of the *differences* between every pair of within-subject levels are equal. Mauchly's test flags violations, but (like Levene's and Shapiro-Wilk) it's sensitive to sample size. The pragmatic move: under *Assumption Checks → Sphericity corrections*, tick Greenhouse-Geisser (or Huynh-Feldt) by default — they adjust the degrees of freedom in the ANOVA table to give you a result that's robust to sphericity violations whether or not Mauchly is significant.
:::

### How to do it in JASP

Go to **ANOVA → Repeated Measures ANOVA**.

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## JASP Video Library — Repeated-Measures ANOVA
Recordings at [jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#anova-repeated](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#anova-repeated):

- [▶ Main Analysis](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#main-analysis-3) — defining within-subjects factors and levels
- [▶ Sphericity Test](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#sphericity-test) — Mauchly's test and Greenhouse-Geisser correction
- [▶ Plots](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#plots-1) — profile plots for within-subjects designs
- [▶ Contrasts](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#contrasts) — planned comparisons
- [▶ Post-Hoc Tests](https://jasp-stats.github.io/jasp-video-library/All_ANOVA.html#post-hoc-tests-1) — pairwise comparisons with correction
:::

---

## Interpreting the ANOVA output

The ANOVA table shows:

| Column | What it means |
|---|---|
| **F** | Test statistic — variance between groups / variance within groups |
| **df** | Degrees of freedom — (number of groups − 1) for the effect, (N − number of groups) for the error |
| **p** | Significance — reject H₀ if *p* < α |
| **η²** or **ω²** | Effect size |

::: {.callout-tip}
## APA reporting
*F*(df_effect, df_error) = [value], *p* = [value], η² = [value].

Example: *F*(2, 57) = 4.32, *p* = .018, η² = .13.

For repeated measures with a correction: *F*(1.76, 46.5) = 5.21, *p* = .011, η² = .17 (Greenhouse-Geisser corrected).
:::

---

## Non-parametric alternatives

If ANOVA assumptions are seriously violated:

| ANOVA design | Non-parametric alternative |
|---|---|
| One-way (between) | [Kruskal–Wallis test](nonparametrics.qmd) |
| One-way (within) | [Friedman test](nonparametrics.qmd) |

Both are available under **ANOVA → Nonparametric** in JASP. See the [Non-Parametric Tests](nonparametrics.qmd) page for the full walkthrough.

---

## Further reading

- **DSUJ Chapter 11** — *One-way independent ANOVA*
- **DSUJ Chapter 12** — *Analysis of covariance (ANCOVA)*
- **DSUJ Chapter 13** — *Factorial ANOVA*
- **DSUJ Chapter 14** — *Repeated-measures designs*

---

## Further viewing — SSR lecture recordings

The 2026 SSR lecture series covers ANOVA across several recordings (captions are available in the SharePoint player):

- [▶ SSR Lecture 12 — ANOVA](https://amsuni-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/j_b_vandoorn_uva_nl/ERes4EOiQZpGiel4rafb1u4B5bqXqGTWv2QiTKTA4xGB9A?e=VSXMW4&nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJTdHJlYW1XZWJBcHAiLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJTaGFyZURpYWxvZy1MaW5rIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXcifX0%3D) — one-way ANOVA, the F-statistic
- [▶ SSR Lecture 14 — ANCOVA](https://amsuni-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/j_b_vandoorn_uva_nl/ER_RAS7DHcFBniFgxHobdoUBLhJX7wJC8obRIgBmG9QCIg?nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJTdHJlYW1XZWJBcHAiLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJTaGFyZURpYWxvZy1MaW5rIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXcifX0%3D&e=KvLQQl) — adding continuous covariates
- [▶ SSR Lecture 15 — Factorial ANOVA](https://amsuni-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/j_b_vandoorn_uva_nl/EYOhbLyuOsZHqJd72rlUp-IBMjUeoS2Z3f_ak44KzW-4Dw?e=ygYJyb&nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJTdHJlYW1XZWJBcHAiLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJTaGFyZURpYWxvZy1MaW5rIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXcifX0%3D) — main effects and interactions
- [▶ SSR Lecture 18 — Repeated-Measures ANOVA](https://amsuni-my.sharepoint.com/:v:/g/personal/j_b_vandoorn_uva_nl/ERu30EipOpFHlscrmrf4PHsBeJft-YNnXKJxuPq1GaEflQ?nav=eyJyZWZlcnJhbEluZm8iOnsicmVmZXJyYWxBcHAiOiJTdHJlYW1XZWJBcHAiLCJyZWZlcnJhbFZpZXciOiJTaGFyZURpYWxvZy1MaW5rIiwicmVmZXJyYWxBcHBQbGF0Zm9ybSI6IldlYiIsInJlZmVycmFsTW9kZSI6InZpZXcifX0%3D&e=OetsEz) — within-subjects designs, sphericity

---

## Try it yourself

One exercise for each of the four ANOVA designs covered above. Each ends with a collapsible solution — try the analysis first, then expand to compare.

### Exercise 1 (one-way ANOVA) — Do the four Hogwarts houses differ in Machiavellianism?

Jakob et al. (2019) measured Machiavellianism in students who identified as Gryffindor, Hufflepuff, Ravenclaw, or Slytherin. Using [`leni_11.jasp`](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_11.jasp), run a one-way ANOVA, follow it up with a **Tukey HSD** post-hoc test, and add a planned contrast comparing Slytherin against the other three houses combined.

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked solution
- **Walkthrough**: [Labcoat Leni — Science behind the magic?](https://discoverjasp.com/pages/labcoat_leni#science-behind-the-magic)
- **Annotated JASP output**: [leni_11.html](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_11.html)
:::

### Exercise 2 (ANCOVA) — Does interpretation training induce a persistent negative bias in children?

Muris et al. (2008) randomly assigned children to one of three training conditions (negative / positive / no information) and measured a later interpretation-bias score. The age, gender, and natural anxiety of each child should be controlled for. Using [`leni_12.jasp`](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_12.jasp), fit an ANCOVA with training as the factor and the three demographics as covariates. Check the homogeneity-of-regression-slopes assumption.

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked solution
- **Walkthrough**: [Labcoat Leni — Space invaders](https://discoverjasp.com/pages/labcoat_leni#space-invaders)
- **Annotated JASP output**: [leni_12.html](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_12.html)
:::

### Exercise 3 (factorial ANOVA) — Does mood × "stop rule" affect checking behaviour?

Davey et al. (2003) used a 2 × 3 design (mood: negative/positive × stop-rule: "as many as can" / "feel like continuing" / no instruction) and measured checking behaviour. Using [`leni_13.jasp`](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_13.jasp), fit a two-way factorial ANOVA — but inspect the **interaction plot before the main effects**. (This dataset is a textbook reminder that main effects can mislead when the interaction is significant.)

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked solution
- **Walkthrough**: [Labcoat Leni — Don't forget your toothbrush](https://discoverjasp.com/pages/labcoat_leni#dont-forget-your-toothbrush)
- **Annotated JASP output**: [leni_13.html](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_13.html)
:::

### Exercise 4 (repeated-measures ANOVA) — Are splattered cadavers distracting during serial recall?

Participants attempted serial recall while three different types of music played (no music / classical / "death metal"). Using [`leni_14.jasp`](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_14.jasp), fit a repeated-measures ANOVA on the music factor, check **Mauchly's test** for sphericity, and apply the **Greenhouse-Geisser** correction if needed.

::: {.callout-note collapse=true icon=false}
## ▸ Worked solution
- **Walkthrough**: [Labcoat Leni — Are splattered cadavers distracting?](https://discoverjasp.com/pages/labcoat_leni#are-splattered-cadavers-distracting)
- **Annotated JASP output**: [leni_14.html](https://discoverjasp.com/repository/dsj1_leni/leni_14.html)
:::
